topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Albright stared at the object resting on his cluttered desk. It was a small, intricately carved wooden bird, painted with startlingly vibrant blues and greens. He hadn't seen Elias in nearly five years, not since graduation, and the sheer unexpectedness of the delivery—a polite, anonymous note slipped under his door—left a strange tremor in his hands. He picked it up, the wood surprisingly warm against his palm. His breath hitched, a shallow catch in his chest. Who would think to send something so personal, so deliberate, after all this time? He turned the bird over and over, the detail of the craftsmanship seeming to mock his professional composure. A faint perspiration beaded on his forehead. He needed to put it somewhere safe, somewhere it wouldn't draw attention, but the impulse to examine it again, to trace the tiny lines of the carving, kept pulling him toward the desk.

[story 2]

"Thank you, Mr. Davies," Mrs. Peterson managed, her voice sounding unnaturally high. The package sat on the welcome mat, a plain brown box tied with twine. Inside, nestled in tissue paper, was a vintage astronomy book she hadn't seen in decades. It was a thoughtful gesture, certainly, but the timing felt wrong. She kept glancing toward the front door, half-expecting someone to materialize. Her fingers tightened around the book, the paper rough beneath her touch. A quick, involuntary swallow dried the sudden dryness in her throat. She felt a prickle running down her spine, a sensation like static electricity building beneath her skin. She tried to focus on the comforting weight of the book, but her attention kept drifting to the hallway, waiting for a sound, a movement, anything that explained this intrusion into her quiet routine.

[story 3]

I found the small, silver locket tucked into the spine of my textbook. It was tarnished, old, and undeniably familiar. It was from Sarah, a student I hadn't spoken to since I left the university. My heart began to beat a little faster, a hurried rhythm against my ribs. I traced the delicate engraving with my thumb, the metal cool against my skin. It wasn't just the gift; it was the implication—that she was still watching, still connected. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair slightly askew. The silence of my apartment seemed amplified, heavy and expectant. I paced the length of the room twice, my gaze sweeping over the windows as if expecting a figure to be standing just beyond the glass. Every instinct screamed at me to check the door, to see if the quiet presence that had just manifested was real.
